12.21: Don't let evil get the upper hand—get the upper hand over evil by doing good.
12.2: Stop being shaped by the mold of this present age—instead, let God reshape you from the inside by renewing your thinking. Then you will be able to discern what God's will actually is: what is good, what pleases him, what is complete.
12.9: Love must be real—no play-acting. Hate evil with everything in you; hold tightly to what is good.
12.19: Dear friends, never take revenge. Leave room for God's wrath—for it is written: 'Vengeance is mine; I will repay, says the Lord.'
12.1: In light of all these mercies of God, I urge you, brothers and sisters, to offer your bodies as living sacrifices—holy and pleasing to God. This is the worship that truly makes sense.
12.13: Share what you have with God's people who are in need. Make hospitality a habit.
12.16: Live in harmony with one another. Don't be too proud for humble company—get alongside those of low status. Never become wise in your own estimation.
12.3: By the grace God has given me I say to each of you: don't overestimate yourself. Form a sober, accurate self-assessment—in line with the measure of faith God has given you.
12.10: Love one another with the deep affection of family. Try to outdo each other in showing respect.
12.5: —so we, though many, form one body in Christ, and each of us belongs to all the others.
12.6: We each have different grace-gifts, given according to what God has given us. If your gift is prophecy, use it in proportion to your faith.
12.20: No—instead: 'If your enemy is hungry, feed him. If he is thirsty, give him a drink. Doing this will pile burning coals on his head.'
12.17: Never pay back evil with evil. Think ahead about what will be seen as honorable by everyone.
12.18: As much as it depends on you, live peaceably with everyone.
12.8: If it is encouraging others, then encourage. If it is giving, give with open-handed generosity. If it is leading, lead with energy. If it is showing kindness, do it with a smile.
12.4: Just as a single body has many parts—and those parts don't all do the same thing—
12.7: If it is serving, then serve. If it is teaching, then teach.
12.14: When people persecute you, bless them—bless, don't curse.
12.11: Don't drag your feet in your work—keep your passion burning, serving the Lord.
12.12: Be full of joy in hope, steady in suffering, persistent in prayer.
12.15: Celebrate with those who are celebrating; grieve with those who grieve.

13.4: The authority is God's appointed servant working for your benefit. But if you are doing wrong, be afraid—the sword is not carried for nothing. That authority is God's agent, executing his judgment on the wrongdoer.
13.3: Rulers are not a threat to people who do good—only to those who do wrong. Want to live without fear of the authorities? Then do what is right, and you will have their approval.
13.9: The commandments—'Don't commit adultery,' 'Don't murder,' 'Don't steal,' 'Don't covet'—and any other commandment you can think of—are all summed up in this one: 'Love your neighbor as yourself.'
13.8: Don't leave any debt unpaid—except the debt of love you owe each other, which you can never fully repay. For the person who loves others has fulfilled the Law.
13.10: Love never wrongs its neighbor. That is why love is the fulfillment of the whole Law.
13.14: Instead, clothe yourselves with the Lord Jesus Christ—and stop making plans to feed the cravings of the flesh.
13.5: So there are two reasons to submit: to avoid punishment, yes, but also because it is the right thing to do before God.
13.2: So whoever resists the authority is resisting God's ordering of things—and those who resist will bring judgment down on themselves.
13.1: Every person should be subject to those in authority over them. There is no authority that does not ultimately come from God—the authorities that exist are placed there by God.
13.7: Pay what you owe everyone: taxes to the tax authorities, revenue to those who collect it, respect to those who deserve respect, honor to those who deserve honor.
13.12: The night is nearly over; the day is about to dawn. So let us strip off the deeds that belong to darkness and put on the full armor of light.
13.13: Let us live as people do in the daylight—no wild parties or drunkenness, no sexual immorality or indecency, no quarreling or envy.
13.6: This is also why you pay taxes—because the authorities are God's public servants, devoting themselves to this work.
13.11: Do all this aware of the hour—it is already time to wake from sleep! Our final salvation is closer now than when we first believed.

14.16: Don't let something you consider good acquire a reputation for evil.
14.15: If what you eat causes your brother or sister pain, you are no longer walking in love. Do not—over a food choice—destroy someone for whom Christ died.
14.17: Because the reign of God is not about what you eat or drink—it is about right living, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit.
14.10: So why do you judge your fellow believer? Or why do you look down on them? We will all stand before God's judgment seat.
14.21: The right thing is to avoid meat or wine or anything else that would cause your brother or sister to fall.
14.13: So let's stop judging one another. Instead, make this your firm decision: never put a stumbling block in a fellow believer's path.
14.20: Don't tear down God's work for the sake of a meal. Everything is clean—but it is wrong to eat in a way that causes someone to stumble.
14.19: So let's go after the things that make for peace and build each other up.
14.4: Who are you to judge someone else's household servant? Whether they stand or fall is their master's business—and they will stand, because the Lord is able to hold them up.
14.23: But if someone eats while having doubts, they are condemned—because their eating does not flow from faith. And anything that does not flow from faith is sin.
14.9: This is precisely why Christ died and rose again—to become Lord over both the dead and the living.
14.18: Whoever serves Christ in these things is pleasing to God and respected by people.
14.8: If we live, we live for the Lord. If we die, we die for the Lord. Whether we live or die, we belong to the Lord.
14.7: None of us lives for themselves alone, and none of us dies for themselves alone.
14.12: So each of us will give a personal account to God.
14.1: Welcome the person whose faith is still fragile—but not in order to argue about their views.
14.2: One person's faith lets them eat anything; another, whose faith is weaker, eats only vegetables.
14.3: The person who eats everything must not look down on the one who doesn't. And the one who abstains must not condemn the one who eats—because God has accepted that person.
14.11: As scripture says: 'As I live, says the Lord, every knee will bow before me, and every tongue will openly acknowledge God.'
14.6: The person who treats a certain day as special does so for the Lord. The person who eats does so for the Lord—giving thanks to God. And the person who abstains also does so for the Lord—and gives thanks to God.
14.14: I am fully convinced in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean in itself. But if someone considers a thing unclean, then for that person it is unclean.
14.22: Whatever you believe about these matters, hold it as a private conviction before God. Happy is the person who has no reason to condemn themselves in what they decide.
14.5: One person regards one day as more sacred than another; another person considers every day the same. Each should be fully satisfied in their own conviction.

16.8: Greet Ampliatus, whom I dearly love in the Lord.
16.9: Greet Urbanus, our fellow worker in Christ, and my dear Stachys.
16.12: Greet Tryphaena and Tryphosa, those hard workers for the Lord. Greet my dear Persis—another woman who has given herself tirelessly to work for the Lord.
16.5: Greet also the congregation that meets in their home. Greet my dear Epaenetus—the very first person in the province of Asia to come to Christ.
16.16: Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the churches of Christ send their greetings.
16.2: Welcome her in the Lord as befits God's people, and give her whatever help she needs from you—for she has been a patron and supporter to many, including me.
16.15: Greet Philologus and Julia, Nereus and his sister, and Olympas—and all of God's people who are with them.
16.1: I want to introduce to you our sister Phoebe, who serves as a minister of the church at Cenchreae.
16.23: Gaius, who has opened his home to me and to the whole church, sends his greetings. Erastus, the city treasurer, and our brother Quartus also send their greetings.
16.17: I urge you, brothers and sisters, to watch out for those who cause divisions and put people off course—acting against the teaching you received. Stay away from them.
16.14: Greet Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, and Hermas—and the brothers and sisters who meet with them.
16.27: to the only wise God—through Jesus Christ—be glory forever and ever. Amen.
16.26: —but has now been brought to light through the prophetic scriptures, at the command of the eternal God, made known to all nations to bring about the obedience of faith—
16.18: People like that are not serving Christ our Lord—they serve their own appetites. With plausible speech and flattery they lead astray the unsuspecting.
16.19: Your obedience is known everywhere—I rejoice over you! But I want you to be wise about what is good, and to remain innocent of what is evil.
16.3: Give my greetings to Prisca and Aquila, my fellow workers in Christ Jesus.
16.4: They put their own necks on the line to save my life. Not just I but every Gentile church owes them gratitude.
16.20: The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet. May the grace of our Lord Jesus be with you.
16.24: May the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with all of you. Amen.
16.7: Greet Andronicus and Junia, my fellow Jews and fellow prisoners—outstanding in the eyes of the apostles, and believers in Christ before I was.
16.10: Greet Apelles, who has proved himself in Christ. Greet the household of Aristobulus.
16.25: Now to the one who has the power to establish you—in line with the good news I proclaim, the message about Jesus Christ, and in line with the unveiling of the mystery that was kept hidden through the ages—
16.11: Greet Herodion, my fellow Jew. Greet those members of the household of Narcissus who belong to the Lord.
16.6: Greet Mary, who worked extremely hard for your community.
16.13: Greet Rufus, chosen by the Lord—and his mother, who has been like a mother to me as well.
16.21: Timothy, my fellow worker, sends his greetings. So do Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater, my fellow Jews.
16.22: I, Tertius, who took down this letter, greet you in the Lord.
